speaker: interviewer
text: Essex, 12-19-95, interviewer, Leah Zozullier, Rochester, New York, USA, English. So by then you'd pretty well found out that your mother and father were-- were dead, and some of your siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't this time. I know the-- where the grave is.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- after--after in Krakow, a couple miles from my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but the little place that you talking, Przemysl, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Przemysl is a different city-- Galicia. See, I know-- because, you see, I was in Slomniki. From Slomniki I go to a farmer who lived over there. And they tell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their neighbor told me, come on. I'll show you where your father and mother is. All right. See over here? Father and the mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And even I go to the camp, it was the Nazis officer over there. Come on. I told her, I can't go with you. You are-- you are a officer, German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't worry about that. Nothing they do. Come on. We go, all right? We go to Slomniki. And after Slomniki, a couple miles from Slomniki, they show me. You see over there? That's a grave with a lot-- a lot of Jewish people. That's your mother over here, and your father's over here, together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I saw the grave. Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they lived over there. They lived over there. They saw everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Slomniki was how far from Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Slomniki, oh, about four hours ago by horses. They had horses over there. Today, I don't know how long it would take.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there you were in Italy. And you couldn't go to Israel, YOU didn't want to go to Australia. By then, you were married, and had a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Napoli. First I was in Cinecitta.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're making movies over there. [LAUGHS] And after this, go-- but first, I come, I was go to Milan. In Milan, they told me, come on over there. And I would go-- I travel a little, a little travel in Italy. Italy was very good. I liked that. In Rome, I was in Rome. I saw-- I saw the Pope, [NON-ENGLISH]. I saw the [NON-ENGLISH]. I told them I don't want your money. I want to go away. No. You not can go away. I can do nothing for you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You see, the Pope that is now is Polish. He's from my hometown. I know him in person.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother tell me, you know this guy? Yeah. He come over to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: He got the book, the Bible. My mother asked you, what you do with them? He wants to be a priest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother told him, you be big if you're a priest. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he lived near you, or he--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He lived a couple miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did he come to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a tailor. Yeah. And after this, he come. My father told me to do something for him, for the fathers. Yeah-- all right. For this time, I know him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was a young boy then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. He's mine age. He's mine age.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother say to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you be a big boy, a big man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's a big man. [LAUGHS] Everybody told me, why you not go to him? I told them, he's an old man. I'm an old man. And go from here to Italy again? No. I be staying in Italy, I was-- I make a petition to stay in Italy. The government told me, you cannot stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: They say, you know the poor people here after the war. I told them. OK. We have to go farther.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you saw the Pope did you talk with him about--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Pope that you saw in Italy, in Rome?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't talk to him about Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. No. I wish I could talk to him. And I can't talk to him. Then nobody let them in. I tell them I talk to him. Oh, but then the Polish Pope was over there, difference. But he come to stand where I was. Oh, he'd be happy. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you couldn't stay in Italy. You wanted to?

speaker: interviewer
text: You wanted to stay in Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wanted to stay there. But the government told no. Because you see, they told me-- I never got work for my people, the Italian people. All right. All right. No problem. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you ended up coming to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I was going? Milan first.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Milan to the United States by boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Napoli-- not Napoli. I forget. New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I came from Napoli over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got to New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They say, the ship it not go yet. I have to wait. I wait another two days. And up, that way we going.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. First I got to New York. And from New York, I get my transport over here because I come over here like a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were sponsored by a relief organization? And as a tailor, where did you work here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hickey Freeman. You know he give me my raise?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. But Hickey Freeman, you see, but Hickey Freeman is Italian people. Most of them were Italian before. Today, I don't know. They're asking me, do you speak Italian? Oh, yeah. Then come on. We're going to speak Italian. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: See, the Italian people over here, they are half Italian. Somebody in Italy? Oh, they're happy. I got over there a foreman, by Hickey. He started to holler.

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman's said, hey, you stop with the holler at this guy. He's our man. He's Italian. [LAUGHS] Well, yeah. It's past.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: About two months. And after, I go someplace else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now why was he hollering at you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He no like if somebody the Italian people you like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: After this I was going Schuler Potato Chips.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed for a while. And after this I go someplace called Gerber's baby food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Making the food or putting it in the jar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Putting it in the jars by the machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Today over here you've got to work, not for nothing money. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was your son when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son was about three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you said you worked at Xerox too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I worked with them about 18 years, 19 years. Good company. Good. They tell me, stay. I tell them, no. Because young people, they want to work. Let them work. I'll go up.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 10, eight, nine. That's enough. They come pretty soon-- they come over here about Friday, Saturday, I got over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Essig, you went through a lot. What does it all mean to you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What's the lesson in it for you or for the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can tell you once the leaves-- a-- a lesson, a lesson. Tell them, the people again-- make ready. Nobody or nothing, just be ready for every minute. You never know what's going to happen over here. I tell you what it is. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No different today-- Jews, the Christians-- because the situation is very bad now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The people now-- oh, no, nothing could be-- All right. For me, I wish them good luck. For me, I not go to the war because I watch.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Second World War. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I wish-- I wish I could do something more for the people. And I can't do nothing more. I'm all done. When I'm younger-- difference. I'm an older man, an old man. That's a problem. It's the whole problem, is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of the things that happened to you and your family, how did you survive them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I do how much I can. I was working. I do like this. I do it. And I said, you see, when you have life to live, no different how you survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the Nazi camp, five years. And come over here to Italy, from Italy over here. And I started working, catch a couple dollars. And like that, come up a little. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then I got friends that come over here. And I tell you, people, they look at Japs. They looking out for Japs. The government have to help them. I-- I not tell the government, help me. No. I don't want it. I work hard for everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're-- you're worried about the world now?

speaker: interviewer
text: You think things can happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope not. I worry for the young people, the young generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what things worry you the most?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the situation is the war, another war. And I don't want the people-- get be killing in a situation like this. People do not care. Tell you the truth. the people-- the American people do not care for that. We people from Europe, we knows. We know the wars with everything. They think it's a playground. It's not a playground. It's a bad. And I told them, stay ready. If you need to go to fight, you have to fight-- nothing you can do. I fight the Germans too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Make it a good army, and make it the guns ready in every minute. If you go wait till the war come up, it will be too late. Same like me-- the war coming up, the German coming up. And right away they took me to the army, and then go with them. You have to. You have to go like that. You have to save the country. It's either this or there will be another one.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the problem. Actually I read the papers, I look like-- You said you liked to go out and go walking around, and go to the cafes for coffee.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do that. I do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You used to do that in Europe also, you said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So that keeps you going, huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you go over to the shops here, do you meet people that you--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you talk about what's going on in the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people. I have got all the friends over there. Tomorrow, Tuesday to Wednesday. Tomorrow Wednesday, right? Tomorrow Irish day. They come over here. We talk to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the synagogue that you go to, the shul is around the corner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. And are you active there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you busy with the shul there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime. The shul is old, about 80 years. You see, this house and the synagogue, it's the same age. Yeah. I remember the church. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a German section. This a German section. This one.

speaker: interviewee
text: They tell me. Over here was a German that lived over here. They are the one selling me this house. So I have no one to sell him his house. And it has been until May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why you don't want to sell this house? Oh, sell or not? All right. I sold this house. Do you feel safe in this neighborhood now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. It's all right. It's up to people-- how the people go. No. It's not bad. It's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I notice you have a lot of stickers on your door from the police and the Army. How did you get them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They send me. Why they send me? Money. I give them. And the police get me on, they hang up. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. Oh, boy, boy, boy, boy boy. Well, let's wait till-- we're still on the same one, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of story for instance?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. What I want to know is-- here we are at the end of the interview. Is there anything else that you want to say or remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go to say again the same to the young people-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to take care of yourself and watch your steps. That's all. OK? Good. Something else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, probably in Galicia they took it.

speaker: interviewer
text: He lived in what little town?

speaker: interviewer
text: Przemysl. And about how old was he when the photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Probably about 70, about 65, 75. Who knows?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And this photograph-- What's this photograph of?

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's you in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me and my wife. And this is the rest of them, all of my friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, wait a minute-- in '51, I come over here. This was about '46-- '47. I think it's about '47 year, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were those friends in the camp that you were in?

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the displaced persons camp? Tell us about this photograph here.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph, they make this in Italy. There was I think so 19-- 1948 or 1947. It's a-- a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now we're going to say it for real. OK. This photograph was taken after the war before you went to Italy and before you were married?

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. OK. Got it. OK. Who is this?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel. I think it's in Israel they make the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was about 60 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows what day they make that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go on a trip to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us about that one. When they tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: This they make in Italy. After the war I was in Italy for work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those are people in a displaced camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I don't know them. I know that we worked together.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that's supposed to be me in the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so that would have been somewhere in the late 1940s?

speaker: interviewer
text: The same one or a different one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Different one. This is the oldest sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ava. She's gone. She's not here no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. Poland. This is the wedding. It's got the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she the one that was in Krakow that you went back to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She was not in Krakow. No. No. [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one? It's my sister-- my daughter. That's my daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think they was married a couple years ago.
